 or cancellation costs. If any of our government contracts were to be terminated for default,
3
Table of Contents
generally the U.S. government would pay only for the work that has been accepted and could require us to pay the difference between the original contract price and the cost to re-procure the contract items, net of the work accepted from the original contract. The U.S. government can also hold us liable for damages resulting from the default.
Commercial Aircraft.
In the U.S., our commercial aircraft products are required to comply with FAA regulations governing production and quality systems, airworthiness and installation approvals, repair procedures and continuing operational safety. For example, as a result of the 737-9 door plug accident in January 2024, the FAA investigated the 737 quality control system, including Spirit AeroSystems Holdings, Inc. (Spirit), and increased its oversight of our production and quality and safety management systems. The 737 program may only increase production rates and/or implement new production lines with the concurrence of the FAA. New aircraft models and new derivative aircraft are required to obtain FAA certification prior to entry into service. Outside the U.S., similar requirements exist for airworthiness, installation and operational approvals. These requirements are generally administered by the national aviation authorities of each country and, in the case of Europe, coordinated by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency.
Environmental.
We are subject to various federal, state, local and non-U.S. laws and regulations relating to environmental protection, including the utilization, discharge, treatment, storage, disposal and remediation of hazardous substances and wastes. We could also be affected by laws and regulations relating to climate change, including laws limiting or otherwise related to greenhouse gas emissions. These laws and regulations could lead to increased environmental compliance expenditures, increased energy and raw materials costs and new and/or additional investment in designs and technologies. We continually assess our compliance status and management of environmental matters to monitor compliance with applicable environmental laws and regulations. Investigation, remediation and operation and maintenance costs associated with environmental compliance and management of sites are a normal, recurring part of our operations. These costs often are allowable costs under our contracts with the U.S. government. It is reasonably possible that costs incurred to ensure continued environmental compliance could have a material impact on our results of operations, financial condition or cash flows if additional work requirements or more stringent clean-up standards are imposed by regulators, new areas of soil, air and groundwater contamination are discovered and/or expansions of work scope are prompted by the results of investigations. For additional information relating to environmental contingencies, see Note 15 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.
Non-U.S. Sales.
Our non-U.S. sales are subject to both U.S. and non-U.S. governmental regulations and procurement policies and practices, including regulations relating to import-export control, tariffs, investment, exchange controls, anti-corruption and repatriation of earnings. Non-U.S. sales are also subject to varying currency, political and economic risks.
Raw Materials, Parts and Subassemblies
We are highly dependent on the availability and quality of essential materials, parts and subassemblies from our suppliers and subcontractors. The most important raw materials required for our aerospace products are aluminum (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions), titanium (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions) and composites (including carbon and boron). Although alternative sources generally exist for these raw materials, qualification of the sources could take a year or more. Many major components and product equipment items are procured or subcontracted on a sole-source basis. We continue to work with a small number of sole-source suppliers to ensure continuity of supply for certain items.
Suppliers
We are dependent upon the ability of a large number of U.S. and non-U.S. suppliers and subcontractors to meet performance specifications, quality standards and delivery schedules at our anticipated costs.
4
Table of Contents
While we maintain an extensive qualification and performance surveillance system to control risk associated with such reliance on third parties, failure of suppliers or subcontractors to meet commitments has and could continue to adversely affect product quality, production schedules and program/contract profitability, thereby jeopardizing our ability to fulfill commitments to our customers. We have also experienced significant increases in supplier prices which are adversely affecting our business. We are also dependent on the availability of energy sources, such as electricity, at affordable prices.
Seasonality
No material portion of our business is considered to be seasonal.
Executive Officers of the Registrant
See "Item 10. Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance" in Part III.
Other Information
Boeing was originally incorporated in the State of Washington in 1916 and reincorporated in Delaware in 1934. Our principal executive offices are located at 929 Long Bridge Drive, Arlington, Virginia 22202, and our telephone number is (703) 465-3500.
General information about us can be found at www.boeing.com. The information contained on or connected to our website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K and should not be considered part of this or any other report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Our Annual Report on Form 10-K, Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q and Current Reports on Form 8-K, as well as any amendments to those reports, are available free of charge through our website as soon as reasonably practicable after we file them with, or furnish them to, the SEC. The SEC maintains a website at www.sec.gov that contains reports, proxy statements and other information regarding SEC registrants, including Boeing.

Forward-looking statements are based on expectations and assumptions that we believe to be reasonable when made, but that may not prove to be accurate.

BA.quantitative_report:
## Boeing (BA) — Quantitative Report, Decision Date 2026-04-01

---

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue:** Quarterly revenue recovered from a trough of $15.24B (Q4 2024) to $22.75B (Q2 2025), representing a +49.3% sequential rebound. Prior year same-quarter (Q2 2024) was $16.87B, implying roughly +35% YoY improvement at the Q2 2025 level.

**Earnings:** Quarterly EPS improved from catastrophic losses (−$9.97 in Q3 2024, −$5.45 in Q4 2024) toward near-breakeven in Q1 2025 (−$0.16), though Q2 2025 reverted to −$0.92. Full-year 2025 data is incomplete; the annual net margin flipped to +2.5% by the valuation snapshot date, suggesting a profitable H2 2025 not fully visible in the supplied quarterly income statements.

**Gross Margin:** Recovered from deeply negative territory (−$3.51B or −19.7% in Q3 2024, −$1.59B or −10.4% in Q4 2024) to positive $2.44B / 10.7% in Q2 2025. Annualized gross margin reported at 4.8% as of the valuation date.

**Operating Margin:** Turned slightly positive in Q1 2025 ($461M / +2.4%), then reverted to −$176M (−0.8%) in Q2 2025. Annual operating margin reported at 4.8%.

**Cash Flow:** No cash-flow statement data supplied. Cash generation trajectory is unobservable.

**Balance Sheet:** Equity swung from deeply negative (−$23.56B in Q3 2024, −$17.02B in Q1 2024) to positive $5.46B by Q4 2025 — a +$29B improvement. Total debt increased from $47.69B (Q1 2024) to $53.85B (Q4 2025). Total assets expanded from $134.48B to $168.24B.

---

### 2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings Quality — Weak:**
- Five of the six supplied quarters show negative EPS; only Q1 2025 approached breakeven (−$0.16).
- The reported annual net margin of 2.5% and ROE of 41% appear to rely heavily on results in H2 2025 quarters not supplied.
- ROE of 41% is mechanically inflated by an extremely thin equity base ($5.46B on $168.24B in assets), not by robust profitability.
- Gross margin of 4.8% and operating margin of 4.8% leave almost no buffer for any cost shock.
- Net margin history over the 365-day window: persistent negative readings (−17.8% to −14.2%) from April 2025 through January 2026, flipping to +2.5% only in the most recent observations.

**Balance-Sheet Resilience — Fragile:**
- Debt-to-equity of 9.868x is extreme; total debt of $53.85B is nearly 10× the $5.46B equity base.
- Liabilities of $162.78B consume 96.8% of $168.24B total assets.
- Equity was negative for at least four consecutive quarters (Q1 2024 through Q2 2025) before turning positive.
- Cash on hand was $10.50B as of Q3 2024 (last available cash figure); no subsequent cash balance supplied.

---

### 3. Valuation Versus Own Supplied History

| Metric | Current (2026-03-31) | 365-Day Range | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E diluted | 80.3x | 76.3–98.7x | Near low end of range, but 80x on razor-thin earnings |
| P/B | 27.8x | 26.4–34.2x | Near low end, but equity base is $5.46B on $168B assets |
| P/S | 1.70x | n/a (single point) | No historical comparison available |
| Earnings yield | 1.2% | n/a | Sub-10Y Treasury yield |
| Net margin | 2.5% | −17.8% → 2.5% (12pts) | Recently turned positive after sustained deep losses |
| ROE | 41.0% | 0.4–0.4 | Reflects thin equity, not high returns on invested capital |
| Debt/Equity | 9.87x | n/a | Single observation; extreme leverage |

Valuation is not cheap in absolute terms: 80× earnings on a 1.2% yield with 2.5% net margins and 9.9× leverage. The lower end of the P/E and P/B ranges still represents historically elevated multiples for this name. The apparent discount to range highs reflects recent price decline (−8.4% quarterly) more than fundamental improvement in profitability.

---

### 4. Price Level and Return Context Versus Fundamentals

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Latest close (2026-03-31) | $199.03 |
| 52-week high | $254.35 |
| 52-week low | $128.88 |
| Distance from high | −21.8% |
| Distance from low | +54.4% |
| Quarterly return (≈63d) | −8.4% |
| Annual return (≈252d) | +14.8% |

The stock rallied +20.9% in Q2 2025 (from $173.31 to $209.53) and +3.6% in Q3 2025, broadly coinciding with the revenue recovery visible in the income statements. The subsequent −8.4% quarterly decline in Q1 2026 coincides with the annual net margin stabilizing at just 2.5% — a level that does not justify 80× earnings. Price has given back roughly half the annual gain, but remains +54% above the 52-week low despite no quarter of positive EPS in the supplied data.

---

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside

**Upside:**
- Revenue trajectory: $22.75B in Q2 2025 vs. $15.24B trough in Q4 2024 (+49.3%); if sustained, operating leverage on a high-fixed-cost base could drive margin expansion.
- Balance-sheet repair: equity improved from −$23.56B to +$5.46B, a $29B swing; further retained earnings could reduce leverage.
- Stock is 21.8% below 52-week high, providing mean-reversion potential if earnings stabilize.

**Downside:**
- Extreme leverage: debt-to-equity of 9.868× means any earnings shortfall or cash burn could quickly return equity to negative territory.
- Earnings fragility: 2.5% net margin leaves zero cushion; the 76.3× floor on the P/E range still prices in perfection.
- No cash-flow data: inability to verify whether operating cash flow supports the thin reported margins.
- Price has declined −8.4% in the most recent quarter while fundamentals show only marginal improvement; momentum is negative.

---

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

The fundamental picture is one of a fragile recovery: revenue has rebounded sharply, but profitability remains marginal (2.5% net margin, 4.8% operating margin) and the balance sheet carries 9.9× debt-to-equity with a $5.46B equity cushion on $168B in assets. Cash flow is unobservable. At 80× earnings, the valuation demands sustained and significant margin expansion from levels that were deeply negative just three to four quarters ago. The absence of any supplied quarter with positive EPS, combined with extreme leverage and a sub-market 1.2% earnings yield, presents unfavorable risk/reward: the upside requires successful execution on margin expansion to justify the current multiple, while the downside is amplified by thin equity and high debt. Price has already retraced 21.8% from highs, but the fundamental base does not yet support the valuation even at the low end of the historical multiple range.
BA.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Assessment Report: Boeing (BA)**
**Decision Date: 2026-04-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Boeing is a major global aerospace company organized into three segments: Commercial Airplanes (BCA), Defense, Space & Security (BDS), and Global Services (BGS). BCA is a leading producer of commercial jetliners (737, 767, 777, 787 families) with development ongoing on the 777X and 737 derivatives. BDS engages in military aircraft, weapons systems, satellites, and space exploration. BGS provides a full spectrum of lifecycle services for both commercial and defense platforms. The business is heavily regulated (FAA, NASA, U.S. military) and faces intense competition from Airbus, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX, among others. Boeing is highly dependent on a large network of suppliers and subcontractors. (Source: 10-K, 2026-01-30)

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Positive Commercial Demand (2026-03-24):** United Airlines announced a major fleet expansion, including Boeing 787-9s and 737 MAX jets, signaling continued strong demand for Boeing's commercial products.
*   **Positive Analyst Sentiment (2026-03-19):** Tigress Financial raised Boeing's price target, citing an "inflection in scale, profitability, cash generation, and demand visibility" based on 2025 results.
*   **Space Program Catalyst (2026-03-15):** NASA's revised Artemis plan involves using Boeing's Space Launch System (SLS) rocket, with standardization to a proven Centaur second stage to reduce costs and increase launch frequency.
*   **Negative Program Setback (2026-03-14):** NASA released a critical 311-page report on Boeing's Starliner spacecraft, detailing failures that nearly resulted in crew loss. NASA has suspended crewed Starliner flights pending major fixes.
*   **Legal Overhang (2026-03-19, 2026-03-27):** A U.S. federal court certified a shareholder class-action lawsuit related to 737 MAX safety disclosures, covering investors from November 2018 to October 2019.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Execution & Quality Control.** The Starliner report (2026-03-14) highlights severe technical and quality failures, damaging reputation and potentially delaying future revenue. The 10-K (2026-01-30) notes FAA increased oversight of 737 production after a 2024 incident, with production rate increases requiring FAA concurrence.
    *   *Offset:* The company's core commercial backlog remains robust, as evidenced by large airline orders (2026-03-24). The 10-K states a focus on improving products, processes, and cost reduction.
*   **Risk: Legal and Regulatory.** The certified class-action lawsuit (2026-03-19) represents a significant ongoing legal and financial overhang. The 10-K (2026-01-30) details heavy regulation and the government's right to terminate contracts for default.
    *   *Offset:* The 10-K notes that costs for environmental compliance are often allowable under U.S. government contracts. The business is diversified across segments and customers.
*   **Risk: Labor Relations.** The 10-K (2026-01-30) documents recent major strikes in 2024 and 2025 that halted production and materially impacted financials, with the risk of future work stoppages.
    *   *Offset:* The 10-K describes ongoing culture transformation and investments in employee development and recognition programs.
*   **Risk: Competitive Pressure.** The 10-K (2026-01-30) identifies aggressive competition in all segments from entities like Airbus, SpaceX, and others. News (2026-03-22) highlights SpaceX's pricing power in the launch market.
    *   *Offset:* Boeing's scale, broad product/service portfolio, and entrenched position in defense and commercial aviation provide a competitive moat.

**4. Qualitative Assessment**
**Cautiously Positive.** The franchise benefits from a duopoly in large commercial aircraft, a large defense portfolio, and a growing services business. Recent evidence shows a strong commercial order book and positive analyst revisions, suggesting an operational inflection. However, this is materially offset by severe, confirmed execution failures in the Starliner program, a significant certified class-action lawsuit, and a history of disruptive labor actions. The near-term narrative is one of recovery and demand strength, but it is tempered by deep-seated operational and legal risks that require sustained management focus to overcome.

unsupported_claims: The quant report states: "The annual net margin flipped to +2.5% by the valuation snapshot date, suggesting a profitable H2 2025 not fully visible in the supplied quarterly income statements." The supplied quarterly data shows all six quarters with negative EPS (Q1 2024: −$0.56; Q2 2024: −$2.33; Q3 2024: −$9.97; Q4 2024: −$5.45; Q1 2025: −$0.16; Q2 2025: −$0.92). No H2 2025 quarterly income statement data is supplied. The claim that the annual net margin is +2.5% is sourced from the valuation ratios (net_margin=0.025 as of 2026-02-26 and 2026-03-31), but the underlying quarterly income statements that would support a profitable H2 2025 are absent from the raw packs. The report infers 'a profitable H2 2025 not fully visible' — this is an inference, not a fact supported by the supplied evidence.

The quant report states: "Gross Margin: Recovered from deeply negative territory (−$3.51B or −19.7% in Q3 2024, −$1.59B or −10.4% in Q4 2024) to positive $2.44B / 10.7% in Q2 2025. Annualized gross margin reported at 4.8% as of the valuation date." The Q2 2025 gross margin of 10.7% is computed as $2.44B / $22.75B = 10.7%, which checks out. However, the report says 'Annualized gross margin reported at 4.8% as of the valuation date' — the raw valuation ratios show gross_margin=0.048, which is consistent. No issue here, but the report's framing of 'annualized' could mislead since the 4.8% figure is a trailing annual metric, not a simple annualization of Q2.

The quant report states: "ROE of 41% is mechanically inflated by an extremely thin equity base ($5.46B on $168.24B in assets)." The raw data confirms total_equity=5.46B and roe=0.41. The report correctly identifies the mechanical distortion. However, the report also states 'ROE of 41% appears to rely heavily on results in H2 2025 quarters not supplied' — this is an inference about H2 2025 profitability that cannot be verified from the supplied quarterly data, since all supplied quarters show negative net income.

The qual report states: "Tigress Financial raised Boeing's price target, citing an 'inflection in scale, profitability, cash generation, and demand visibility' based on 2025 results." The raw qualitative evidence confirms this from the 2026-03-19 web context. However, the Tigress article references 'cash generation' — the supplied raw quantitative evidence contains no cash flow data whatsoever (all cash flow entries show 'no data'). The qual report is citing a third-party claim about cash generation that cannot be verified from the supplied packs.

The qual report states: "Boeing's fourth quarter and full-year 2025 results showed an inflection in scale, profitability, cash generation, and demand visibility, according to Tigress Financial. The company posted impressive revenue growth of 34." This is from the web context. The 34% revenue growth figure is not verifiable from the supplied quarterly data — the latest supplied quarterly income statement is Q2 2025, and Q4 2025 and full-year 2025 data are not in the raw packs. The qual report is relying on external analyst claims about financial results not present in the supplied evidence.

The qual report mentions 'The 10-K (2026-01-30) notes FAA increased oversight of 737 production after a 2024 incident, with production rate increases requiring FAA concurrence.' The raw 10-K text confirms: 'as a result of the 737-9 door plug accident in January 2024, the FAA investigated the 737 quality control system... and increased its oversight of our production and quality and safety management systems. The 737 program may only increase production rates and/or implement new production lines with the concurrence of the FAA.' This is accurately cited.

The qual report states: 'During 2024, employees represented by IAM District 751... went out on strike for 53 days' and 'During 2025, employees represented by IAM District 837... went out on strike for 101 days.' The raw 10-K text confirms both figures. Accurately cited.

The quant report states: "Total debt increased from $47.69B (Q1 2024) to $53.85B (Q4 2025)." The raw balance sheet confirms total_debt=47.69B for 2024-03-31 and total_debt=53.85B for 2025-12-31. Accurate.

The quant report states: "Equity swung from deeply negative (−$23.56B in Q3 2024, −$17.02B in Q1 2024) to positive $5.46B by Q4 2025 — a +$29B improvement." The raw data confirms total_equity=-23.56B (2024-09-30), -17.02B (2024-03-31), and 5.46B (2025-12-31). The swing from -23.56B to +5.46B is $29.02B. Accurate.

The quant report states: "Cash on hand was $10.50B as of Q3 2024 (last available cash figure); no subsequent cash balance supplied." The raw balance sheet shows cash=10.50B only for 2024-09-30, with no cash figures for subsequent periods. Accurate.

The qual report's 'Cautiously Positive' assessment references 'a strong commercial order book and positive analyst revisions, suggesting an operational inflection.' The order book strength is supported by the United Airlines news (2026-03-24) and AerCap deal (2026-03-24) in the raw evidence. The analyst revision is supported by the Tigress Financial item. However, the Tigress claim about 'profitability' and 'cash generation' inflection cannot be verified from supplied financials.

Omitted evidence: The raw qualitative evidence includes a 2026-03-19 news item about Brent crude spiking to $119/barrel, which could materially affect Boeing's input costs and airline customer demand. Neither specialist report mentions this macro risk factor that is present in the supplied evidence packs.

Omitted evidence: The raw qualitative evidence includes the 2026-03-27 news about the smart weapons market growing to $31.89B by 2031 with 7.45% CAGR, which is relevant to Boeing's BDS segment. The qual report does not mention this potential tailwind despite it being in the supplied evidence.

Internal contradiction: The quant report's conclusion states 'the absence of any supplied quarter with positive EPS' — this is accurate per the raw data. However, the report simultaneously accepts the annual net margin of 2.5% as valid for valuation purposes (80x P/E), while also noting the quarterly data doesn't support profitability. The report should more explicitly flag that the P/E ratio itself may be unreliable if the annual earnings figure is driven by unsupplied H2 2025 data of unknown quality.

- Omitted evidence: The raw qualitative evidence includes the 2026-03-27 news about the smart weapons market growing to $31.89B by 2031 with 7.45% CAGR, which is relevant to Boeing's BDS segment. The qual report does not mention this potential tailwind despite it being in the supplied evidence.
- Internal contradiction: The quant report's conclusion states 'the absence of any supplied quarter with positive EPS' — this is accurate per the raw data. However, the report simultaneously accepts the annual net margin of 2.5% as valid for valuation purposes (80x P/E), while also noting the quarterly data doesn't support profitability. The report should more explicitly flag that the P/E ratio itself may be unreliable if the annual earnings figure is driven by unsupplied H2 2025 data of unknown quality.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Boeing is a genuine turnaround story with improving revenue and a $29B equity swing from deeply negative to positive, but the recovery remains fragile: no supplied quarter shows positive EPS, the balance sheet carries 9.9x debt-to-equity, and the stock at 80x earnings on a 1.2% yield demands near-perfect execution from a company still plagued by Starliner failures and class-action litigation. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Boeing is a genuine turnaround story with improving revenue and a $29B equity swing from deeply negative to positive, but the recovery remains fragile: no supplied quarter shows positive EPS, the balance sheet carries 9.9x debt-to-equity, and the stock at 80x earnings on a 1.2% yield demands near-perfect execution from a company still plagued by Starliner failures and class-action litigation. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels.
key_factors: Revenue recovery is real but profitability remains razor-thin: Q2 2025 revenue of $22.75B (+49.3% from Q4 2024 trough of $15.24B) and annual net margin of 2.5%, yet all six supplied quarterly income statements show negative EPS, with the most recent Q2 2025 at -$0.92.

Balance sheet repair is significant but fragile: equity swung from -$23.56B (Q3 2024) to +$5.46B (Q4 2025), a $29B improvement, but debt-to-equity of 9.87x means any earnings shortfall could quickly erode the thin equity cushion.

Valuation demands sustained margin expansion: at 80x earnings (P/E) and 1.2% earnings yield, the stock prices in perfection; the 21.8% decline from the 52-week high of $254.35 to $199.03 reflects some de-rating but not enough given the fundamental fragility.

Qualitative risks are material: NASA's critical 311-page Starliner report (2026-03-14) suspended crewed flights, a certified class-action lawsuit (2026-03-19) creates legal overhang, and FAA oversight constrains 737 production rate increases, partially offset by strong commercial orders like United Airlines' fleet expansion (2026-03-24).

Competition and the Health Care Environment
Competition
The markets in which the Company conducts its business and the pharmaceutical industry in general are highly competitive and highly regulated. The Company's competitors include other worldwide research-based pharmaceutical companies, smaller research companies with more limited therapeutic focus, generic drug manufacturers, and animal health care companies. The Company's operations may be adversely affected by generic and biosimilar competition as the Company's products mature, as well as technological advances of competitors, industry consolidation, patents granted to competitors, competitive combination products, new products of competitors, the availability of generic or biosimilar versions of competitors' branded products, and new information from clinical trials of marketed products or post-marketing surveillance. In addition, patent rights are increasingly being challenged by competitors, and the outcome can be highly uncertain. An adverse result in a patent dispute can preclude commercialization of products or negatively affect sales of existing products and could result in the payment of royalties or in the recognition of an impairment charge with respect to intangible assets associated with certain products.
6
Table of Contents
Pharmaceutical competition involves a rigorous search for technological innovations and the ability to market these innovations effectively. With its long-standing emphasis on research and development, the Company is well-positioned to compete in the search for technological innovations. The Company is active in acquiring and marketing products through external alliances, such as licensing arrangements and collaborations, and has been refining its sales and marketing efforts to address changing industry conditions. However, the introduction of new products and processes by competitors may result in price reductions and product displacements, even for products protected by patents. For example, the number of compounds available to treat a particular disease typically increases over time and can result in slowed sales growth or reduced sales of the Company's products in that therapeutic category.
The highly competitive animal health business is affected by several factors including regulatory and legislative issues, scientific and technological advances, product innovation, the quality and price of the Company's products as well as competitors' products, effective promotional efforts and the frequent introduction of generic products by competitors.
Health Care Environment and Government Regulation
Global efforts toward health care cost containment continue to exert pressure on product pricing and market access. Changes to the U.S. health care system as part of health care reform, as well as increased purchasing power of entities that negotiate on behalf of Medicare, Medicaid, and private sector beneficiaries, have contributed to pricing pressure. In several international markets, government-mandated pricing actions have reduced prices of generic and patented drugs. In addition, the Company's sales performance in 2025 was negatively affected by other cost-reduction measures taken by governments and other third parties to lower health care costs. In the U.S., the Executive Branch and Congress continue to discuss legislation designed to control health care costs, including the cost of drugs. The Company anticipates all of these actions and additional actions in the future will continue to negatively affect sales and profits.

MRK.quantitative_report:
## MRK Quantitative Report — 2026-04-01

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue** is modestly growing on a YoY basis: Q4 2025 revenue of 16.40B vs. Q4 2024 of 15.62B = +5.0% YoY. TTM revenue stands at 65.01B. However, QoQ revenue declined −5.1% (from 17.28B in Q3 2025). The quarterly revenue series shows a relatively narrow band of 15.53B–17.28B over the trailing eight quarters, indicating stable but lumpy top-line without a clear acceleration trend.

**Earnings** are deteriorating on a TTM basis despite revenue growth. TTM net income is 18.26B. Q4 2025 EPS of 1.20 represents a −48.3% QoQ decline from Q3's 2.32 and a −18.9% YoY decline from Q4 2024's 1.48. Net income fell −20.9% YoY in the latest quarter (2.96B vs. 3.74B).

**Margins** are compressing sharply in Q4 2025: gross margin fell to 66.2% (10.85B / 16.40B) versus Q3's 77.7% (13.42B / 17.28B). Cost of revenue spiked to 5.55B in Q4 vs. 3.85B in Q3 — a 44% QoQ increase. Operating income dropped to 3.42B (20.9% operating margin) from Q3's 6.75B (39.1%). TTM gross profit is 48.63B (74.8% margin) and TTM operating income is 21.07B (32.4% margin).

**Cash flow** data is entirely unavailable across all reported periods. Free cash flow, operating cash flow, and investing/financing flows are unreported.

**Balance sheet** expanded significantly: total assets grew from 117.11B (Q4 2024) to 136.87B (Q4 2025) = +16.9%. Total equity rose from 46.37B to 52.66B = +13.6%. A data anomaly is present: total liabilities are reported as negative in Q2 and Q1 of both 2025 and 2024 (e.g., −49.06B in Q2 2025), which is inconsistent with the Q3/Q4 positive figures and likely reflects a reporting or data-extraction error. Excluding those periods, liabilities grew from 70.73B (Q4 2024) to 84.20B (Q4 2025) = +19.0%, outpacing asset growth.

### 2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality concerns:** The Q4 2025 gross margin of 66.2% is a significant outlier versus the seven prior quarters (range: 74.0%–77.7%). This cost-of-revenue spike (5.55B vs. the trailing average of ~3.75B) warrants caution — it may indicate a one-time charge, inventory write-down, or structural cost increase. Without cash-flow data, accruals cannot be isolated, leaving earnings quality partially opaque.

**Balance-sheet resilience:** The equity base of 52.66B on 136.87B of assets implies a leverage ratio (assets/equity) of 2.60x. The debt-to-equity ratio (total liabilities / total equity) is 1.60x (84.20B / 52.66B). ROE remains strong at 34.7% per the latest valuation window. The reported balance-sheet anomalies (negative liabilities) make granular leverage analysis unreliable for several quarters; only Q4 2024 and Q3/Q4 2025 provide consistent liability data.

### 3. Valuation Versus Own History

**P/E:** The latest P/E of 16.52 is at the top of the 365-day window (range: 10.7–17.0). Over the 12-point sparse history, P/E expanded from 12.9 (April 2025) to 16.5 (March 2026), a +28% multiple expansion. The P/E was as low as 11.26 in June 2025.

**P/B:** At 5.73, this is near the top of the 3.8–6.0 range and has expanded from 4.77 in April 2025 (+20%).

**P/S:** At 4.64, TTM sales of 65.01B imply a market cap near 302B.

**Earnings yield:** 6.1% — modestly above risk-free rates but compressed versus history given the multiple expansion.

**Net margin** has declined from 0.296 (Nov 2025–Jan 2026) to 0.281 (latest), while ROE slipped from 0.367 to 0.347, reflecting the Q4 earnings deterioration.

**Key tension:** The stock is priced at historically elevated multiples while earnings momentum is negative. The P/E expansion from ~11–12x to ~16.5x has occurred alongside a decline in EPS from 2.14 (Q2 2024) to 1.20 (Q4 2025). This is multiple expansion against deteriorating fundamentals — a potential risk setup.

### 4. Price Level and Return Context

**Price:** 120.29 (close 2026-03-31). The 52-week range is 73.31–125.14; the stock is within 4% of its high.

**Quarterly return:** +12.8% (Q1 2026), following +35.7% in Q4 2025, −0.7% in Q3 2025, and −11.3% in Q2 2025.

**Annual return:** +34.8% (from ~89.23 in late March 2025).

**Interpretation:** The stock has nearly doubled from its trough of 73.31 (within the 365-day window), driven largely by multiple expansion (P/E from ~11x to ~16.5x) rather than earnings growth. The Q4 2025 fundamental deterioration (EPS −48.3% QoQ, −18.9% YoY) has not yet been reflected in price — indeed, the stock rallied 12.8% in the quarter following that disclosure.

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside

**Upside:**
- If the Q4 2025 margin compression (gross margin 66.2% vs. trailing ~75%) proves transitory and margins revert, TTM earnings could recover toward ~7.50+ EPS (annualizing Q1–Q3 2025 run-rate), implying significant earnings recovery.
- ROE of 34.7% and TTM net margin of 28.1% (on a full-year basis) demonstrate underlying profitability strength if the Q4 cost spike is non-recurring.
- Revenue growth of +5.0% YoY in Q4 suggests the franchise is still expanding.

**Downside:**
- The P/E of 16.5x is at the ceiling of its 365-day range while earnings are declining; mean reversion of the multiple to the 12–13x center (consistent with most of 2025) would imply ~25–30% downside if earnings do not recover.
- Q4 gross margin erosion (66.2% vs. 75%+ trend) may signal structural cost increases, permanently impairing the earnings trajectory.
- The balance-sheet anomaly (negative reported liabilities in multiple quarters) prevents reliable leverage monitoring and may mask hidden risks.
- Total liabilities grew 19.0% YoY (Q4 2024→Q4 2025), outpacing asset growth of 16.9%, indicating rising financial risk.

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

MRK presents a **cautious risk/reward profile** as of 2026-04-01. The stock has undergone substantial multiple expansion (P/E from ~11x to 16.5x, +50%) and price appreciation (+34.8% annual, +64% from trough) while Q4 2025 fundamentals showed material deterioration: EPS fell 48.3% QoQ and 18.9% YoY, gross margin collapsed to 66.2%, and operating income halved from the prior quarter. Cash-flow data is absent, preventing independent verification of earnings quality.

The valuation is now at the top of its historical range while the earnings trajectory is at its weakest. This creates asymmetric risk: multiple compression toward the 12–13x historical center — even at stable earnings — implies 20–25% downside. Upside requires both margin reversion and sustained multiple expansion, a less certain proposition. **The quantitative balance skews to the downside**, contingent on whether the Q4 cost structure is confirmed as a one-time event or a new baseline.
MRK.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Research Report: Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK)**
**Decision Date: 2026-04-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Merck is a global healthcare company with two reportable segments: **Pharmaceutical** and **Animal Health**. The Pharmaceutical segment is dominated by the immuno-oncology franchise, led by **Keytruda** (2025 sales: $31.68B), and includes vaccines (e.g., Gardasil), hospital acute care, and cardiometabolic products. The Animal Health segment is a diversified business ($6.35B in 2025 sales) serving livestock and companion animals. The company's strategy emphasizes innovation, external alliances, and pipeline development to address growth beyond Keytruda.

**2. Dated Recent Developments**
*   **2026-03-25:** Merck announced an agreement to acquire **Terns Pharmaceuticals** for **$6.7 billion ($53/share)** to add TERN-701, a novel CML therapy, to its pipeline. The deal is explicitly framed as addressing Keytruda's **patent expiry in 2028**.
*   **2026-03-25:** Analyst commentary (from a separate news snippet) suggests the Terns deal **may attract rival bids**, indicating potential competitive pressure on the acquisition.
*   **2026-03-24:** Merck entered a **multi-year research collaboration with Quotient Therapeutics** for inflammatory bowel disease, valued at up to **$2.2 billion** including milestones.
*   **2026-02-24:** The **10-K filing** details extensive recent product approvals expanding Keytruda into new indications and geographies (e.g., ovarian cancer in Feb 2026, bladder cancer in Nov 2025), as well as approvals for new products like Winrevair and Welireg.
*   **2026-02-16 & 2026-02-19:** Web analyses post-Q4 2025 results note management provided **"better growth visibility"** for the post-Keytruda loss of exclusivity (LOE) period, citing a long-term revenue target exceeding **$70 billion by the mid-2030s**.
*   **2026-03-03 & 2026-03-08:** Stock price rose **~42% over six months** (trading near $120), with market commentary attributing strength to **confidence in the oncology franchise and pipeline visibility**, alongside its status as a **stable dividend stock** (P/E ~16, yield ~2.8%).

**3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets**
*Risks:*
*   **Keytruda Patent Cliff (2028):** The single largest risk is the upcoming loss of exclusivity for Keytruda, which represented ~49% of 2025 pharmaceutical sales. The Terns acquisition highlights management's urgency to address this.
*   **Acquisition & Pipeline Execution:** The Terns deal is for an early-stage asset (Phase 1/2), and there is noted **risk of rival interest**. Broader pipeline success in new therapeutic areas (e.g., PAH, hematology) is uncertain.
*   **Competitive & Pricing Pressure:** The 10-K explicitly cites **intense competition**, generic/biosimilar threats, and **global health care cost containment** as ongoing pressures on pricing and volume.
*   **Regulatory and Legal Environment:** Acknowledged risks include **patent challenges, government regulations, and litigation**. A separate note references an ongoing **trademark dispute with Merck KGaA**.
*   **Momentum Sustainability:** Recent stock appreciation may already reflect optimism, creating valuation sensitivity to any pipeline setbacks.

*Offsets:*
*   **Diversified Growth Drivers:** Beyond Keytruda, the portfolio includes fast-growing newer drugs like **Winrevair (PAH)** and **Welireg (onc)**, a stable vaccine franchise, and a non-cyclical Animal Health business.
*   **Active Business Development:** Recent actions (Terns acquisition, Quotient collaboration) demonstrate a **proactive strategy to replenish the pipeline** through external innovation.
*   **Strong Current Momentum:** The **42% stock gain** and positive commentary follow **better-than-expected Q4 2025 results** and an improved growth outlook, signaling improved near-term confidence.
*   **Financial Stability:** Described as a **"stable dividend stock"** with a **30+ year dividend history**, the company offers income appeal amidst its growth transition.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral, with a Negative Bias.** Merck’s core franchise faces an undisputed major cliff in 2028. Recent aggressive M&A and collaboration activity are direct, necessary responses but carry significant execution and integration risks. While the company has a strong current position, improved near-term visibility, and diverse assets, the weight of the impending Keytruda patent expiry—the central fact in the evidence—creates a material qualitative headwind. The assessment hinges on the success of its post-Keytruda strategy, which remains unproven.

corrections.issue: The qualitative report states Keytruda represented '~49% of 2025 pharmaceutical sales.' The 10-K shows Keytruda sales of $31,680M on total pharmaceutical sales of $58,142M, which is 54.5%, not 49%. The 49% figure would only be correct if measured against total company sales ($31,680M / $65,011M = 48.7%). The phrasing 'pharmaceutical sales' is therefore inaccurate — it should say 'total company sales' or the percentage should be ~54.5% of pharmaceutical sales.
corrections.required_action: Clarify whether the 49% figure refers to total company sales (correct) or pharmaceutical segment sales (incorrect). The distinction matters because the Keytruda cliff risk is even larger relative to the pharmaceutical segment than the report implies.

corrections.issue: The qualitative report describes the Q4 2025 results as 'better-than-expected' citing web context, but the quantitative report documents severe Q4 deterioration: EPS -48.3% QoQ, gross margin collapsed to 66.2% from ~75%+ trend, operating income halved. These two characterizations are in tension. The 'better-than-expected' framing likely refers to consensus estimates (not in the evidence packs), while the quant report is comparing to prior quarters. Neither report reconciles this apparent contradiction for the reader.
corrections.required_action: Note that 'better-than-expected' is sourced from external commentary and may reflect consensus estimate beats, not sequential improvement. The quant report's Q4 deterioration data stands on its own and should not be implicitly dismissed by the qual narrative.

corrections.issue: The quantitative report states 'P/E expansion from ~11–12x to ~16.5x, +50%.' The sparse history shows P/E ranged from 11.258 (June 2025) to 16.523 (March 2026). The expansion from 11.26 to 16.52 is +46.7%, and from the ~12x center to 16.5x is ~37.5%. The '+50%' claim is a rounding-up that overstates the expansion slightly. More importantly, the report says 'P/E from ~11x to 16.5x' in the conclusion but the April 2025 starting point was 12.9x, not 11x — the 11x level was only touched briefly in June 2025.
corrections.required_action: Minor: tighten the P/E expansion characterization. The range is 11.3x–16.5x (+46.7%), not '+50%'. The ~11x reference cherry-picks the trough rather than the starting observation.

unsupported_claims: : The qualitative report's claim that Keytruda represented '~49% of 2025 pharmaceutical sales' is inconsistent with the 10-K data in the raw evidence. Keytruda sales of $31,680M divided by pharmaceutical segment sales of $58,142M equals 54.5%. The 49% figure only works against total company sales ($65,011M).

: The qualitative report's characterization of Q4 2025 results as 'better-than-expected' is sourced from web commentary but directly contradicts the quantitative evidence of severe Q4 deterioration (EPS -48.3% QoQ, gross margin 66.2% vs ~75% trend). Neither report reconciles this tension.

: The quantitative report's claim of '+50%' P/E expansion from '~11x' overstates the move. The sparse history shows the trough was 11.26x (June 2025) and the starting observation (April 2025) was 12.9x. The actual expansion to 16.5x is +46.7% from the trough or +28% from the April 2025 starting point.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: MRK presents a classic tension between a stretched valuation and a structurally important but uncertain pipeline transition. The stock has undergone significant multiple expansion (P/E from ~12.9x to 16.5x) while Q4 2025 fundamentals deteriorated sharply, yet the market is pricing in confidence in the post-Keytruda strategy. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels — not compelling enough to chase, but not broken enough to short.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: MRK presents a classic tension between a stretched valuation and a structurally important but uncertain pipeline transition. The stock has undergone significant multiple expansion (P/E from ~12.9x to 16.5x) while Q4 2025 fundamentals deteriorated sharply, yet the market is pricing in confidence in the post-Keytruda strategy. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels — not compelling enough to chase, but not broken enough to short.
key_factors: Valuation at ceiling of historical range: P/E of 16.52x is at the top of the 365-day window (10.7–17.0), having expanded +28% from the April 2025 starting point of 12.9x, while Q4 2025 EPS fell 48.3% QoQ to $1.20 and gross margin collapsed to 66.2% from the ~75% trend — multiple expansion against deteriorating earnings creates asymmetric downside risk.

Keytruda concentration risk is acute: Keytruda sales of $31.68B represent 54.5% of pharmaceutical segment sales ($58.14B) and ~49% of total company revenue, with patent expiry in 2028 creating a structural cliff that the $6.7B Terns acquisition (Phase 1/2 asset) and $2.2B Quotient collaboration are attempting to address but with significant execution uncertainty.

Revenue stability provides a floor: TTM revenue of $65.01B with Q4 2025 YoY growth of +5.0% and a narrow quarterly band of $15.5B–$17.3B over eight quarters, plus a 30+ year dividend history yielding ~2.8%, supports the stock as a defensive holding even amid earnings volatility.

Q4 2025 margin compression is a key variable: the gross margin drop to 66.2% (from ~75% trend) and operating income halving to $3.42B may reflect a one-time charge or structural cost increase — if transitory, TTM earnings could recover toward $7.50+ EPS; if permanent, the current multiple is unjustified.

 do business. In the United States, many of our products and manufacturing operations are subject to one or more federal or state regulatory agencies, including the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC). We are also subject to anti-corruption laws and regulations, such as the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, and antitrust and competition laws and regulations that govern our dealings with suppliers, customers, competitors and government officials.
In addition, many foreign jurisdictions in which we do business have regulations and regulatory bodies that govern similar aspects of our operations and products, in some cases to an even more significant degree. We are also subject to expanding laws and regulations related to environmental protection and other sustainability-related matters, non-financial reporting and diligence, labor and employment, trade, taxation and privacy and data protection, including the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation and similar regulations in states within the United States and in countries around the world.
The Company has in place compliance programs and internal and external experts to help guide our business in complying with these and other existing laws and regulations that apply to us around the globe; and we have made, and plan to continue making, necessary expenditures for compliance with these laws and regulations. We also expect that our many suppliers, consultants and other third parties working on our behalf share our commitment to compliance, and we have policies and procedures in place to manage these relationships, though they inherently involve a lesser degree of control over operations and governance. We do not expect that the Company's expenditures for compliance with current government regulations, including current environmental regulations, will have a material effect on our total capital expenditures, earnings or competitive position in fiscal year 2026 as compared to prior periods.
Human Capital
. Our employees are a key source of competitive advantage. Their actions, guided by our Purpose, Values and Principles (PVPs), are critical to the long-term success of our business. We aim to retain our talented employees by offering competitive compensation and benefits, strong career development and a respectful and inclusive culture that provides equal opportunity for all.
Our Board of Directors, through the Compensation and Leadership Development Committee (C&LD Committee), provides oversight of the Company's policies and strategy relating to talent, including equality and inclusion, as well as the Company's compensation principles and practices. The C&LD Committee also evaluates and approves the Company's compensation plans, policies and programs applicable to our senior executives.
Employees
As of June 30, 2025, the Company had approximately 109,000 employees, an increase of 2% versus the prior year due to business growth. The total number of employees is an estimate of total Company employees excluding interns, co-ops, contractors and employees of joint ventures. 49% of our employees are in manufacturing roles and 28% of our employees are located in the United States. 42% of our global employees are women and 32% of our U.S. employees identify as multicultural.
Training and Development
We focus on attracting, developing and retaining the broadest pool of talent available, both from universities and the broader market. We recruit from universities across markets in which we compete and are generally able to select from the top talent. We focus on developing our employees by providing a variety of job experiences, training programs and skill development opportunities. Given our develop-from-within model for staffing most of our senior leadership positions, it is particularly important for us to ensure holistic growth and full engagement of our employees.
Equality and Inclusion
As a global consumer products company, P&G serves consumers around the world with operations in approximately 70 countries. We believe that it is good for business that our workforce is drawn from the best available talent from communities worldwide, with insights about, connectivity to and understanding of all our consumers. Further, a foundational component of P&G's strategy and success has been to foster an inclusive work environment, in which we develop and advance the very best capabilities that all our people have to offer. Globally, we aspire to reflect the diversity of the consumers we serve in the communities where we operate and are committed to creating a superior employee experience for all. Our aspiration is founded on our longstanding values and principles for equal opportunity and compliance with the law.
Compensation and Benefits
Market-competitive compensation and reward programs are critical elements of our employee value equation to attract and retain the best talent. Our total rewards programs are based on the principles of paying for performance, paying competitively versus peer companies that we compete with for talent in the marketplace and focusing on long-term success through a combination of short-term and long-term incentive programs. We also offer competitive benefit programs, including retirement plans and health insurance, in line with local country practices, with flexibility to accommodate the needs of a diverse workforce.
Sustainability
. Environmental sustainability is integrated into our business strategy. We are focused on designing and manufacturing irresistibly superior products that are more sustainable. We aim to reduce our own environmental footprint and
The Procter & Gamble Company        3
enable our consumers to reduce their footprint without compromising on the performance of the products they use. We develop and license technologies that can be used across industries to improve environmental sustainability at a broader scale.
Combined, this approach intends to positively impact the total environmental impact of the Company while driving market growth and value creation.
In 2021, the Company announced a 2040 net zero ambition. Our Climate Transition Action Plan outlines the Company's ongoing efforts toward reducing greenhouse gas emissions across scopes 1 and 2 and elements of scope 3. The Company has also declared ambitions towards purchasing renewable electricity for our operations, reducing intensity of virgin petroleum-based plastic in packaging, designing more consumer packaging to be recyclable or reusable, responsible sourcing of key forest-based commodities, improving efficiency of water usage in our operations and driving a global portfolio of water restoration projects in key water basins. While we have met or exceeded some of the goals and are making significant progress against others, scalable infrastructure and cost-effective solutions are not yet available to us to fully deliver against some of our stated ambitions.
Sustainability related disclosures included in this Annual Report, our Proxy Statement and our sustainability reports are informed by standards and guidelines such as the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) and the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD). The "materiality" thresholds in those standards and guidelines may differ from the concept of "materiality" for purposes of the federal securities laws and disclosures required by the Commission's rules in this Annual Report. References to our sustainability reports and website are for informational purposes only and neither the sustainability reports nor the other information on our website is incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K. Additional detailed information on our sustainability efforts can be found on our website at https://pginvestor.com/esg.

**Quantitative Report: PG (Decision Date 2026-04-01)**  

1. **Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**  
   - **Revenue**: Latest quarterly (2025-12-31) revenue was 22.21B, with QoQ decline of -0.8% and YoY growth of +1.5%. TTM revenue (sum last 4Q) was 86.25B. Quarterly revenue ranged from 19.78B (2025-03-31) to 22.39B (2025-09-30), showing no consistent uptrend.  
   - **Earnings**: Latest EPS diluted was 1.78, down QoQ by -8.7% and YoY by -5.3%. TTM net income was 17.56B. Quarterly EPS ranged from 1.27 (2024-06-30) to 1.95 (2025-09-30).  
   - **Margins**: Supplied gross margin was 0.513, operating margin 0.238, net margin 0.186 (stable per valuation ratios). Calculated from latest income statement: gross profit margin ≈ 51.2% (11.37B/22.21B), operating margin ≈ 24.2% (5.37B/22.21B), net margin ≈ 19.5% (4.33B/22.21B).  
   - **Cash-Flow**: Unavailable for all periods (all quarters marked n/a).  
   - **Balance Sheet**: Total assets grew from 119.60B (2024-03-31) to 127.29B (2025-12-31). Total equity increased from 50.33B to 53.32B. Liabilities to equity ratio: 73.97B/53.32B ≈ 1.39 (latest). Equity grew steadily but slower than liabilities.  

2. **Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**  
   - **Earnings Quality**: Without cash-flow data, direct quality assessment is limited. Quarterly net income and EPS show volatility with recent declines (-9.4% QoQ, -7.0% YoY). Margins appear stable (net margin range 0.185–0.200 per historical valuations), but earnings trajectory is downward.  
   - **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Equity base of 53.32B versus liabilities of 73.97B indicates moderate leverage. Equity increased by 3.0B over the period (from 50.33B to 53.32B), but liabilities rose by 4.7B (from 69.26B to 73.97B), marginally straining the equity cushion.  

3. **Valuation Versus Company's Own History**  
   - Latest PE diluted: 22.707, at the lower end of the 365-day range (21.7–27.2). Historical sparse data shows PE declining from 26.925 (2025-04-01) to 22.707 (2026-03-31).  
   - Latest PB: 6.734, near the low of its range (6.4–8.2); historically, PB was 8.15 in April 2025.  
   - Earnings yield: 0.044 (implied by PE). Margins and ROE stable (net margin 0.186, ROE 0.297 per supplied ratios). Valuation contraction aligns with earnings decline, but current multiples are below historical medians.  

4. **Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context**  
   - **Price**: Latest close (2026-03-31) was 144.44, within a 52-week range of 137.62–174.80.  
   - **Returns**: Quarterly delta (≈63 trading days) was -0.1%; annual delta (≈252 trading days) was -14.0%. Quarterly path shows consistent declines: from 168.03 (2025-03-28) to 144.44, a cumulative loss of ~-14% over four quarters.  
   - **Versus Fundamentals**: Annual EPS decline of -5.3% (YoY) and revenue growth of +1.5% (YoY) are less severe than the price decline of -14.0%, indicating PE contraction (from implied ~27 to ~22.7). Price near 52-week low reflects weaker investor sentiment despite stable margins.  

5. **Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**  
   - **Upside**: PE at 22.707 is close to the historical low of 21.7; a reversion to the median PE (~25, based on sparse history) would imply ~10% price upside from a valuation perspective. Stable margins (gross margin 0.513) and moderate balance sheet provide a floor.  
   - **Downside**: Continued EPS declines (QoQ: -8.7%) could compress PE further. Liabilities-to-equity ratio of 1.39 and absence of cash-flow data increase financial risk. If earnings deteriorate, price could test the 52-week low of 137.62 (≈5% below current).  

6. **Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**  
   The stock exhibits lower valuation multiples relative to history (PE 22.707 vs. range 21.7–27.2), but earnings trajectory is weak (EPS decline of -5.3% YoY) with no cash-flow evidence. The annual price return of -14.0% exceeds the fundamental EPS decline, suggesting potential for valuation-based upside if earnings stabilize. However, balance-sheet leverage (liabilities/equity 1.39) and lack of cash-flow data present downside risks. Quantitatively, risk/reward is balanced, contingent on earnings durability.
PG.qualitative_report:
### **Qualitative Assessment Report: Procter & Gamble (PG)**
**Decision Date:** 2026-04-01
**Evidence Cutoff:** 2026-03-31

#### **1. Business/Franchise Read**
The Procter & Gamble Company is a world-leading multinational consumer goods company operating in approximately 180 countries (10-K, 2025-08-04). Its business model is built on creating, manufacturing, and marketing a diversified portfolio of daily-use, trusted, branded products. The company focuses on driving balanced top- and bottom-line growth by achieving "irresistible superiority" across product performance, packaging, brand communication, retail execution, and value. It invests in R&D and consumer insights, utilizes a wide range of retail and e-commerce distribution channels, and relies on productivity improvements to fuel investments and value creation (10-K, 2025-08-04). The franchise is characterized by strong brand loyalty, a leadership position in competitive markets, and a 69-year history of consecutive dividend increases, positioning it as a defensive "Dividend King" (News, 2026-03-14; 2026-03-15).

#### **2. Recent Narrative Developments (Dated)**
*   **Corporate Actions & Capital Allocation:** The company's status as a Dividend King with 69 consecutive years of dividend increases is repeatedly highlighted as a key investment attribute for income and stability (News, 2026-03-14; 2026-03-25).
*   **Product & Innovation:** Analysts are citing AI-powered product development as a potential growth catalyst, and the company is extending its brands into new usage occasions, as seen with its anti-dandruff franchise (Web Context, 2026-02-17; News, 2026-03-15).
*   **Guidance & Outlook:** P&G has reaffirmed its guidance for fiscal year 2026, projecting organic sales growth (Web Context, 2026-02-19). Investor presentations and strategic shifts have contributed to renewed optimism and share momentum (Web Context, 2026-02-27).
*   **Competition:** The company operates in highly competitive markets against both global branded products and private-label brands (10-K, 2025-08-04). News reports note its brands face competition but retain strong loyalty (News, 2026-03-20).

#### **3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Product Liability & Litigation.** The company faces multiple class-action lawsuits and regulatory scrutiny, including an investigation into potential securities law violations (2026-03-11), lawsuits related to Old Spice product contamination and recalls (2026-03-26), and allegations that its sleep aid gummies exceeded advertised dosages (2026-03-29). These pose reputational and financial risks.
    *   *Offset:* P&G maintains compliance programs and internal/external experts to navigate regulations (10-K, 2025-08-04). Its diversified brand portfolio and scale may mitigate the impact of issues in any single category.
*   **Risk: Input Cost & Supply Chain Inflation.** Rising oil prices and potential disruptions (e.g., Strait of Hormuz closure) are identified as threats that could increase costs for ingredients, packaging (polyethylene), and transportation, potentially eroding margins (News, 2026-03-22; 2026-03-26).
    *   *Offset:* The company sources a substantial variety of materials globally and notes that changes in commodity prices may be passed to customers, indicating some pricing power (10-K, 2025-08-04).
*   **Risk: Geopolitical & Macro Sensitivity.** Operations across ~180 countries expose it to varied regulations, trade policies, and economic conditions, including inflationary pressures on consumer budgets (News, 2026-03-20).

#### **4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral-to-Positive.** The evidence supports P&G's enduring franchise strength, underscored by its global scale, iconic brands, consistent dividend growth, and reaffirmed operational guidance for FY26. Growth catalysts like AI integration and strategic brand extensions provide a constructive narrative. However, this is counterbalanced by tangible near-term risks from active litigation across multiple brands and potential margin pressure from volatile commodity costs. The stock is consistently cited as a defensive holding, but its outlook is nuanced by these specific, documented operational and legal challenges.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Quarterly net income and EPS show volatility with recent declines (-9.4% QoQ, -7.0% YoY)' for net income. The raw data confirms QoQ net income decline of -9.4% and YoY of -7.0%. However, the report earlier says 'Latest EPS diluted was 1.78, down QoQ by -8.7% and YoY by -5.3%' — these are the EPS figures, not net income. The -9.4% and -7.0% are net income figures, not EPS. The report conflates the two in the earnings quality section by saying 'Quarterly net income and EPS show volatility with recent declines (-9.4% QoQ, -7.0% YoY)' — these percentages are net income declines, not EPS declines (EPS declines were -8.7% QoQ and -5.3% YoY).

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: PG trades near historical valuation lows (P/E 22.7x, P/B 6.7x) with stable margins and a reaffirmed FY26 guidance, but persistent earnings weakness (EPS -5.3% YoY, -8.7% QoQ), active multi-brand litigation, and commodity cost headwinds create enough offsetting risk to keep the risk/reward roughly balanced.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: PG trades near historical valuation lows (P/E 22.7x, P/B 6.7x) with stable margins and a reaffirmed FY26 guidance, but persistent earnings weakness (EPS -5.3% YoY, -8.7% QoQ), active multi-brand litigation, and commodity cost headwinds create enough offsetting risk to keep the risk/reward roughly balanced.
key_factors: Valuation near historical floor: P/E of 22.707 (2026-03-31) sits at the low end of its 1-year range (21.7–27.2), with P/B of 6.734 also depressed versus its range (6.4–8.2), providing a valuation cushion but reflecting justified earnings concerns.

Earnings trajectory weakening: Latest quarterly EPS of $1.78 (2025-12-31) declined -5.3% YoY and -8.7% QoQ, reversing the prior quarter's +21% YoY rebound, with TTM net income of $17.56B and no cash-flow data to confirm earnings quality.

Active litigation across multiple brands: Class-action lawsuits and regulatory investigations span Old Spice contamination (2026-03-26), sleep aid gummies dosage allegations (2026-03-29), and securities law violations (2026-03-11), posing ongoing reputational and financial risk.

Defensive franchise and dividend durability: 69 consecutive years of dividend increases, reaffirmed FY26 organic sales growth guidance (2026-02-19), and stable gross margins (~51.2%) underpin the quality floor, but are insufficient to catalyze re-rating without earnings stabilization.

Increase geographic reach.
By extending our go-to-market capabilities globally, we aim to grow our business by selling to new customers in new regions. We will continue to pursue businesses of all sizes in most major markets globally. We also plan to continue to develop distribution channels through new and existing marketplaces and partners for our solutions around the globe and new go-to-market strategies. We continue to invest in our domestic and international operations and infrastructure to deliver the highest-quality service to our customers around the world.
Focus on industries and new products.
As part of our growth strategy, we are delivering innovative and value-driven solutions in new categories based on our existing and potential customers' needs. For example, we provide out-of-the-box solutions specifically built for customers in certain industries, such as financial services, healthcare and life sciences, manufacturing and more. In addition, through direct discussions and strategic engagements with our customers, we are able to deliver the innovations and enhancements that align with the needs of our customers.
Leverage our partner ecosystem.
The Agentforce 360 Platform enables customers, independent software vendors ("ISVs") and third-party developers to create, test and deliver cloud-based applications. These applications can be marketed and sold on the AppExchange, our enterprise cloud marketplace, or sold directly by software vendors. In addition, we rely on our consulting partners to deliver technology solutions and expertise to customers, from large-scale implementations to more limited solutions that help businesses run more efficiently. We continue to work with and invest in our partner ecosystem, including these ISVs and system integrators ("SIs"), to accelerate our reach into new markets and industries.
Promote strong customer adoption and reduce customer attrition.
We believe that we have the people, processes and proven innovation to help companies transform successfully. Our customer success programs, including success management resources, advisory services, technical architects and business strategists, help enable and accelerate our customers' digital

transformations. In addition, we have free, curated resources such as Trailhead to help companies learn our systems and a community of Trailblazers who drive innovation. With these programs and resources, we aim to reduce attrition and secure renewals of existing customer subscriptions.
Mergers and Acquisitions and Strategic Investments
We evaluate opportunities to acquire or invest in complementary businesses, services, technologies and intellectual property to complement our organic innovation and advance the development of our Agentforce 360 Platform. Our evaluation seeks to confirm that any potential acquisition accelerates our strategy, represents an attractive customer opportunity, provides a pathway to effectively monetize the acquired products and drive significant operational efficiencies and presents a clear timeline for value accretion. Our acquisitions can range in size and complexity, from those that enhance or complement existing products and accelerate development of features to large-scale acquisitions that result in new service offerings. Our goal is to prioritize the use of our balance sheet, through cash and debt, to complete acquisitions.
We also manage a portfolio of strategic investments in both privately held and publicly traded companies focused primarily on enterprise cloud companies, technology startups and system integrators. Our investments range from early to late stage companies, including investments made concurrent with a company's initial public offering. We invest in companies that we believe are digitally transforming their industries, improving customer experiences, helping us expand our solution ecosystem or supporting other corporate initiatives, including AI. We plan to continue making these types of strategic investments as opportunities arise that we find attractive, including investments in companies representing targeted businesses, technological initiatives and geographies. Our strategy includes growing our strategic investment portfolio, in part, by reinvesting proceeds from the sales of strategic investments.
Technology, Development and Operations
We primarily deliver our solutions as highly scalable cloud computing application and platform services on a multi-tenant technology architecture. Multi-tenancy is an architectural approach that allows us to operate a single application instance for multiple organizations, treating all customers as separate tenants who run in virtual isolation from each other. This approach allows us to spread the cost of delivering our services across our user base and scale our business faster than traditional software vendors while focusing our resources on building new functionality and enhancing existing offerings.
We provide our services through cloud computing platform partners who offer Infrastructure-as-a-Service, including servers, storage, databases and networking, as well as through infrastructure designed and operated by us but secured within third-party data center facilities. We continue to invest in and expand the deployment of Hyperforce, which allows our platform and applications to be delivered rapidly and reliably to locations worldwide and provides our customers autonomy and control over data residency.
Our technology and product efforts are focused on improving and enhancing the features, functionality, performance, availability and security of our existing service offerings, as well as developing new features, functionality and services. We also remain focused on integrating businesses, services and technologies from acquisitions. Performance, functional depth, security, usability, ease of integration and configuration and sustainability of our solutions influence our technology decisions and product direction.
Competition
The market for our service offerings is highly competitive, rapidly evolving and fragmented, and subject to changing technology with low barriers to entry, shifting customer needs and frequent introductions of new products and services.
Our current competitors include:
- vendors of packaged business software, as well as companies offering enterprise applications delivered through on-premises offerings from enterprise software application vendors and cloud computing application service providers, either individually or with others;
- AI-native companies and emerging startups that leverage generative AI and large language models as the core foundation of their architecture, offering highly specialized, autonomous, or automated solutions that may bypass traditional business process workflows or displace established user interfaces;
- software companies that provide their product or service free of charge as a single product or when bundled with other offerings, or only charge a premium for advanced features and functionality, as well as companies that offer solutions that are sold without a direct sales organization;
- vendors who offer software tailored to specific services, industries or market segments, as opposed to our full suite of service offerings including suppliers of traditional business intelligence and data preparation products, integration software vendors, marketing vendors, e-commerce solutions vendors, or AI software and service vendors;

**Quantitative Report: CRM (Salesforce) as of 2026-04-01**

**1. Trajectory (Revenue, Earnings, Margins, Cash Flow, Balance Sheet)**
- **Revenue**: Grew from $9.32B (Q2 2024) to $10.24B (Q2 2025), a +9.9% increase over four quarters. Latest available income statement is Q2 2025; Q3 and Q4 2025 (ending 2025-10-31 and 2026-01-31) data are not provided.
- **Earnings**: EPS diluted increased from $1.47 to $1.96 over the same period, +33.3%.
- **Margins**: Gross margin stable at ~78% (e.g., 7.99/10.24 = 0.780 in Q2 2025). Operating margin ~22.8% (2.33/10.24). Net margin ~18.5% (1.89/10.24). Latest valuation ratios (2026-03-31) show gross margin 0.777, operating margin 0.201, net margin 0.18.
- **Cash Flow**: No data available for any period.
- **Balance Sheet**: Total assets increased from $92.18B (2024-07-31) to $112.31B (2026-01-31), +21.8%. Total equity grew from $57.63B to $59.14B, +2.6%. Total debt rose from $8.43B to $14.44B, +71.3%. Debt-to-equity ratio increased from 0.146 to 0.244.

**2. Earningsings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
-- **Earnings Quality**: No cash flow data to assess cash conversion. Earnings growth and margin stability are positive, but quality is unquantifiable without cash flow.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Debt-to-equity of 0.244 is moderate but increased. Interest coverage and liquidity ratios are unavailable. Total liabilities of53.16B, total debt $14.44B (27. of total equity). Debt increase may reflect acquisitions or investments, but cash flow impact is unknown.

**3. Valuation vs. Company History**
